steps.title: Connect capital to import type
steps.content: The assertion is about limited capital resolving during the 2000s. Processing with assembly does not require firms to pay for raw materials, while processing with inputs does. A rise in processing with inputs would therefore support the idea that firms had more capital available.

steps.title: Use the change over time
steps.content: The graph shows processing imports with inputs rising from about 650 hundred million dollars in 2000 to about 2,300 hundred million dollars in 2006. Processing with assembly rose much less, from about 250 to about 750 hundred million dollars.

steps.title: Eliminate weak comparisons
steps.content: Choice A makes a 2006 ranking claim that conflicts with the graph because ordinary imports were still greater. Choice C says both processing categories stayed below ordinary imports, but that does not show capital constraints resolving. Choice D compares different years and categories without showing the key rise. The sharper rise in processing with inputs gives the needed support. The answer is B.
